title: 2025 12 02 - FEC (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-02 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Reserve Bank of New Zealand [Part 2]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Reserve Bank of New Zealand
description: Tuesday 2 December 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Reserve Bank of New Zealand Hearing of evidence: Reserve Bank of New Zealand Dr Anna Breman, Governor Rodger Finlay, Board Chair Kate Le Quesne, Assistant Governor Enterprise Services John McDermott, Assistant Governor Operations Karen Silk, Assistant Governor Money Angus McGregor, (Acting) Assistant Governor Financial Stability Nick McBride, General Counsel
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Reserve Bank of New Zealand. Reserve Bank of New Zealand. Dr Anna Breman, Governor

chunks.text: And congratulations on your new role. We, of course, know that you started yesterday and we were just talking, uh, amongst ourselves that, um, we welcome, uh, any comments that we, you may, uh, want to talk about the broader context, uh, of, of, of the en of the environment, uh, of the challenges of the Reserve Bank, of its operational.

We are not going to, uh, uh, stick you to the financial year of, uh, June 30th, 2025. So, um, if you do have any, uh, introductory comments, uh, we, we certainly welcome them. Uh, and, and, uh, feel free to, um, extend those, uh, we're not gonna be so welcome.

Thank you, chair. Um, and committee. Thank you very much. Um, uh, and in terms of opening remarks, the Reserve Bank has achieved a great deal towards the delivery of our mandate functions this year, including navigating challenging economic conditions. So, I'm sure this committee is very aware.

Number one, consumer price inflation has fallen back to within the monetary policies target band of one to three. Uh, the bank has also made importantly, significant progress against our financial stability mandate. We strengthened our regulatory and supervisory approach through the implementation of the Deposit Takers Act 2023, including the publication of decisions on the core standards for deposit takers.

And we will be announcing, uh, I'm happy to tell you, uh, before the year end, our decisions on the core standards for capital. We also launched the deposit compensation scheme midyear, and a really important initiative, uh, with enhanced financial stability by increasing, uh, the protection to individuals from the failure of any individual bank up to a hundred thousand dollars per institution.

We all also instigated our cash community trials. Uh, this is a really important piece of work in providing us insights in how we can provide solutions to community cash issues, particularly in rural areas. I do, however, chair acknowledge, uh, there have been a number of challenges for the Reserve Bank.

This year. We've had resignations of two governors and a chair. Uh, we responded by conducting an extremely robust governor appointment process with over 300 candidates identified, uh, and down to a long list of 30, uh, and eventually five.

And then we have sitting on my right, our new governor, uh, this period of, uh, challenge, uh, has, has required, um, the board to provide additional support to management throughout the year, uh, which we have done. Uh, but once again, we feel moving into 2026 and with our new governor in place, the board will be able to get back to doing what a good board does.

Uh, the bank, uh, has also embarked, uh, uh, as has been well covered in the media on an organizational change and restructure process that's reduced our head count count, uh, from 688 employees to 554, a reduction of 134 people. Um, however, I'm delighted to be able to introduce, uh, uh, to the committee, our new Reserve bank Governor, Dr.

Anna Breman, on her second day. Uh, I will hand over to Dr. Braman soon, uh, for her to make some remarks to the committee. Um, Dr. Braman, of course, you may be aware, was previously the first deputy governor of the Swedish Rex Bank and brings strong central banking expertise through her extensive experience.

Joining us also today, uh, are members of our Reserve Bank executive leadership team. On my left, I have Kate McCain, assistant Governor for Enterprise Services, and John McDermott, assistant Governor for operations. Also on hand, should we all need them. Uh, Karen Silk, who you would've heard from last week, uh, and Angus McGregor, who is acting assistant governor for financial stability.

So, with that, welcome to day two, Anna. Thank you. Thank you, Roger. Uh, and Maria Na, chair and Committee. I am delighted to have the opportunity, uh, to be here today, uh, on my second day, like you pointed out. Um, and I want to listen to you and answer your questions.

My priority in this few weeks before Christmas will be to meet with my team internally, but also with key external stakeholders such as yourself. The Third Bank of New Zealand is an excellent institution, and that is known and it's well regarded globally. As the chair said, reserve Bank has shown resilience in a challenging environment, both globally and internally.

The bank staff are loyal and dedicated, and they work hard to enable prosperity and economic wellbeing for all New Zealanders. Key focus for the bank under my leadership, will be to stay laser focused on our core mandate, and that is low and stable inflation, a stable financial system, and the safe and efficient payment system.

And importantly, that includes ensuring that cash is available to all New Zealanders. As we head into 2026, transparency, accountability and clear communication will be our focus to maintain trust and credibility, uh, with New Zealanders. The Reserve Bank does rank high in international rankings of transparency, but there is more to be done.

So to conclude, I just want to stress that I very much look forward, uh, to having a productive relationship with the committee. Oh, thank you very much. Thank you. And can I just acknowledge you, uh, Roger Finlay for your elevation to chair in August, uh, after being our deputy chair. Uh, so congratulations to that.

And of course to, uh, mem new members of the board and new members of the Monetary Policy Committee. So, I'm gonna go to, uh, the Honorable Barbara Edmonds, Uh, and welcome, um, governor to, uh, both New Zealand and to your new, uh, problem school. Be nice to today, today, but we have a very good respectful relationship here across the tables.

Um, so probably these questions are probably best for, um, Mr. Chair, if that's okay. Uh, Roger and it, and it's really, it has been quite a tumultuous period for the Reserve Bank, and we, and we welcome your comments around Mr. Stability and focus on, on your actual core business. Um, so Mr. Chair, my, my question goes back to the original funding bid, which was within this particular period.

So the original funding bid was for I think, more than $1 billion over the five years, uh, which treasury then obviously had significant concerns around, or significant value for money concerns. And, and then eventually when the government didn't agree to that 1 billion agreed to, um, it's probably there's a lot of perception out there that the board dropped the ball in relation to both the funding bid and then obviously subsequent actions that have resulted with both the governor resigning and the board chair resigning.

Um, why, uh, why did you approve the $1 billion funding bid in the first place then just to subed or, you know, step back to the seven 50? Well, um, there's a very easy, uh, part to the answer, which is, in my experience, commercially, um, any long initiative budget process starts high and ends lower, not the other way around.

Yeah. But I'm not for a minute suggesting that we sort of threw a speculative billion dollars in the year and then settled lower. The detailed answer is, uh, in the initial phases, so we're now, uh, 14 months before the due date of the new five year agreement. Mm-hmm. We were getting oral communications from Treasury and others that the baseline would be 24 25 annual spend minus the requested and suggested 7.5%.

With that guidance, we started shaping up our very first templates of what it looked like. Yeah. The board became involved when we wanted more granularity of, rather than just gross spend, we wanted more granularity in terms of the divisional spend. Mm-hmm. And then in our third iteration, we started looking at what does it spend per function, per statutory responsibility that split it along outcome lines.

So it was a very long process. Um, at the end, and leading to the second part of your question, uh, it was absolutely apparent that our then governor truly felt that the billion dollars was the, um, was the, um, required amount to run the bank and all its functions.

The board in further discussions with treasury and with our own internal staff, perceived and realized a lower number. So then as we got very close to the due date, um, in I believe March, uh, of 2025, we were shaping around Fox seven 50 number. So, so it's been quite clearly reported that that was ultimately that and, and the review of the capital requirement rules were the reason why the, the governor did resign in the end.

Um, in relation to the functions or the work programs, what has the bank had to change as a result of the initial funding bid not being approved? So, um, you know, what, what programs have you had to postpone or cancel as a result of that? And also, how can you, how can you assure the public that and give the confidence that the bank has the expertise that it needs in order to, to actually achieve its functions or to actually meet its functions, given that turnover in staff? Yeah.

Uh, look, absolutely. We knew at our core, and I touched on in my opening address, are statutory requirements. Our, our big rocks of what we need to deliver. Uh, we're at the core of the way we reshaped the business. And then as we got deeper and deeper into budgeting exercise, we realized a lot could be ca gained by the reordering of our priorities.

So if, if you try and identify them, what are, what are the things you were doing that you are not now doing? There aren't that many. It's the pace with which we intended to do them, and the granularity and the depth of some of the more peripheral activities we were doing, uh, you know, how, how deep we were gonna go in those areas.

Uh, do we do a stage one approach rather than a full commissioning of something. So it really was, uh, I thought it was an excellent budgeting exercise led by our former chair, Neil, quickly, um, and, and we, I I hand on heart. I think there might be two or three things that were on our, uh, on our, um, uh, statement of performance, um, yeah.

That we're not now doing. John, could you give further granularity on that? Yeah, sure. I mean, the, one of our key design principles when we entered into the restructure was to prioritize our resourcing around our central banking functions, again, to make sure that we were, we had sufficient resources allocated to those, uh, key output classes and, uh, mandate areas.

So in, in terms of the restructure, we really focus looking for savings across our support lines, uh, support functions in the bank. And that's where we've taken, uh, the largest, um, I suppose, resource reductions or head count reductions. Can you just give an example of, or, or actually list what they are those support functions, and what are the peripherals that the chair referenced?

Yeah. So our support functions are, are, are, are categorized as, um, the functions we use across the bank to enable sort of enterprise activities. So technology, hr, uh, security, uh, finance. Also, our communications area is another example. Uh, the teams that look after things like, uh, risk management and so forth.

And, sorry, um, one last question, Mr. Chair. Um, Mr. Uh, Mr. Chair, Mr. Chair, Roger. Um, there will be a lot of cri criticism of the board over the last year about both your handling of both resignations funding. There will be a lot of criticism in relation to that. I'm just giving you an opportunity now.

Can you provide assurance to the New Zealand public that the focus now, that the board before that you stand by your decision board as both the board member and as a now as board chair, and provide assurance to the public now around the board's capability in relation to the oversight of the Reserve Bank? I'm just giving you the opportunity to do that now. Mm-hmm.

Look, I wouldn't have taken the job if I couldn't answer that question. We have an excellent board, um, my board style for those who might have worked with me. They know I, I chair a board. I don't chair an organization. We have extremely talented people. We have a former governor and Grant Spencer with Susan Patterson, who spent a nine or 10 years on the electricity authority, very competent and regulated space.

Byron Pepper, a 21 year veteran of Goldman Sachs. Um, so, you know, it is a very capable board. We're doing a lot of our work through board committees, and we're powering up committees in case it, it it, for an example, our brand new financial policy committee that will come into effect early next year. Uh, it was working yesterday on the capital review and the capital settings that we'll be announcing before Christmas.

I have total confidence in the fact that the board can deliver the mandates that the government has given us. And, sorry, the supplementary to that then is also in relation to the personnel changes. I mean, it's quite significant. They're two very senior financial leaders in any other country. It's, you know, it'll be significant.

Do you stand by your actions and your statements in relation to that whole period as to the resignation of both the board chair and the governor? I can, I can stand by the fact that the process as very senior officials left was robust, uh, and all risks were assessed in, in the execution of those, uh, uh, requirements.

What I can also say is, um, to lose two governors, uh, and a chair is indeed very unusual. Mm-hmm. Um, but each case was totally different. Uh, thanks to the help of the Ombuds office, it's very clear Adrian's rationale and the reasons why he left.

That's, that's in the public domain in terms of Christian who left, um, you know, uh, he didn't get the role as governor and he wants to pursue other opportunities. And he goes with our very, very best wishes. Um, I hope he won't mind me saying he fully participated in the process to be governor.

Um, and, and in the case of Neil, um, Neil is a very good friend of mine. I've worked with him all this time on the Reserve Bank. He was an outstanding chairman, uh, and he made his resignation decision on his own. Sorry. Can I have one? Yeah, yeah. One more. That's okay. So given you've, you've just confirmed that Neil Quickly was a good friend of yours.

Yep. Are you very, are you confident that there were no biases in relation to some of the judgment calls that were made over the last period where there was, there was no biases attached to that. You mean, you've just confirmed he was a good friend. Yeah. And we've, we've seen in other government agencies very recently where good friendships get into the way of good judgment. Ah, Yeah. Um, I just need to understand your, yeah. So It's making your, you still feel confident that the processes you followed were, were, um,

were followed appropriately despite that you had a close relationship with the Absolutely. Okay. I've gotta stop here from Dan, but What Thanks, Mr. Chair on that. Um, it's on the subject of groupthink. 'cause I think that if you consider the other ministries that I think my, the Honorable member is referring to mm-hmm.

And there is equal criticisms of the Reserve Bank around groupthink. Yeah. What assurances can you give the public in New Zealand that that level of groupthink will change, will dissipate, and that the Reserve Bank is entering a new era of contestability of freedom of thought, uh, and freedom of expression at the most senior levels of the bank. Mm-hmm.

Very good. Well, well, let's start first with the monetary policy committee. We have three outstanding external members coming to the other big rock of our A ambitions as the bank, of course, is financial stability for everyone in New Zealand. We are now bringing two international experts onto that financial new financial policy committee.

So in terms of group think, we have a lot of external think coming into us. Also, I've watched with great pride, the reshaping of our senior leadership team. They are four very talented professionals. I don't see any group thing. I see really strong debate as chair. I get the opportunity to sit in some of the internal committee meetings, and I see that debate.

So I don't see group. Okay. That's loud and clear. Supplementary. I could just bring you into this, um, governor, and again, welcome. Maybe you could just outline what is your style as a leader, uh, in encouraging open, uh, debate and being challenged, et cetera? That's a great question.

And I think that, uh, in a central bank, it is imperative that you have room to have a good discussion. That people are allowed diversity and thought, but it's not just that they're allowed it, they should be encouraged. And you can do it in different ways. But one way that we can do it is to have through, for example, the forecasting process for the monetary policy decisions.

You can encourage people to actually formally take the opposite view, just to make sure that you get both sides of the argument. Um, and throughout the years that I worked in central banking, I've actually written, I, I've given a speech where I discussed the risk of group thinking central banking. So I feel very comfortable that I will be able to encourage a good discussion, both in terms of the financial, uh, stability mandate, but also in terms of the monetary policy mandate.

And research clearly shows that the monetary policy decisions are better made when they're done by a committee where there is diversity of thought. Okay. Thank you. I'm just go into one more sub from Nancy Lou. Thank You chair, and welcome. Congratulations. Welcome to New Zealand. Just to follow up on that, if we are talking about better transparency, allowing diversified opinions, will New Zealand's, new Zealanders get to see the individual decisions coming out from the NPS by the different members?

Because at the moment, we only see one overall decision, but do we get to see the decisions about the Yeah, this is a very good question as well. And like, let me say that I would like to discuss this with the Monetary Policy committee. Okay. Given that it's my second day, I have not had the chance to meet with all of them yet. Good reminder. Yeah.

But this is one of the things that I will bring up for discussion for sure. Thank you, Kevin. Thank you. Thank you. And of course, the establishment of the Financial Policy Committee was attributed, all attributed in your press release to this fine committee here with our banking inquiry. So we'd like to think we played a part in that. Uh, Dr. Deborah Russell. Um, thank you, Mr. Chair.

Um, just to follow on a little bit from what Nancy was talking on, in some ways I actually quite like that we just get the committee decision, not the individual ones. 'cause it takes us away from individual members being lobbied and voted and things like that. But that's a, a decision that you'll want to take on board anyway, as others said, welcome. And, um, uh, this committee has regular sessions with the governor of the Reserve Bank, and actually they're often the most interesting, um, meetings we have as we get to talk about.

He can mix some policy with you. Um, and so we're looking forward to that. Um, February, I think will be our first one. Uh, but, um, I think one of the things we'd like to understand from you as we go into the next monetary policy statement and so on, is, um, your view of the relationship between fiscal policy and monetary policy and the role that you might see monetary policy having, um, in terms of promoting economic growth and so on.

Just to get a little bit of a, a flavor from you as to where you sit on those sorts of issues. And Yeah. Thank you. Uh, that is a very difficult question. Sorry. Uh, no, no, it's perfectly fine. You should ask difficult questions. That's your job. Um, no, but this is the case. Uh, our mandate is very clear that we should focus on keeping inflation low and stable.

Um, and that is what we actually can achieve with monetary policy using our main tool, which is the OCR. Uh, then of course, uh, the overall development in the general economy is also affected by fiscal policy, but fiscal policy is responsibility of Parliament and the government.

So all I really want to stress today is that our focus will be on inflation, but growth of course also affects how inflation involves. But what we can really achieve as the monetary policy committee and that we are mandated to do is to keep inflation low and stable, taking in consideration what's happening at the overall.

So thank you. Um, uh, I, I appreciate that. That's the Reserve Bank's mandate is the emphasis on inflation until quite recently, um, the Reserve Bank had a dual mandate around, um, what do we call it, maximum sustainable employment or something like that as well. Um, so, uh, and that's obviously, um, well, we all regard it as being important for people to be in jobs.

Um, can you say what sort of role you see that it's now no longer a dual mandate, but obviously it's something that will be important to the Reserve Bank. Do you have any views on the relationship between employment and inflation and the pace that we can use both to promote growth? I think what I'm trying to avoid is trying to say too much ahead of the next monetary policy meeting in February, given that there was just a meeting last week, and I was not part of that meeting.

But let me phrase it like this, that everything that goes on in the economy, uh, can affect inflation in different ways. So of course, the labor market is important, and we will clearly, uh, follow the labor market, wage growth, uh, productivity, all of these factors. Uh, but again, the core mandate is to keep inflation low and stable, taking consideration the overall performance of the economy. I think One of the, um, ongoing concerns we have, um, as members of the Labor Party, um, is around employment.

Um, it sort of, I we're deeply concerned that, um, employment gets sacrificed in order to keep inflation down. It is an ongoing concern for us. Dr. Russell, If I could, you know, when you had the monetary policy committee here last week, I was very pleased that they talked in detail with the committee about the, the so-called box in the report about, we were talking about a low hire, low fire environment.

So even though employment's not specifically in the remit anymore, uh, I'm sure the experts and PhDs around, it's impossible to think about inflation without considering unemployment. And there's the evidence in the NPS last week, you know, it's quite a detail based on employment conditions. Oh, I agree. But I like, I, I totally agree.

I I do not, I do understand the economic relationship, I guess, um, uh, in, in, in terms of us. I know other people, we actually do want people to be in jobs. It's a hard thing to do if inflation is pushing us in a different direction. Anyway, that's a We'll see how, how, Yeah. We've got a sub from, uh, Dan. Oh, no.

Oh, you didn't have a sub. Okay, Barbara. So, so then really just talking about jobs and the restructure that you've had to do, result of the funding, uh, decrease. Uh, uh, a note on your responses to questions, I think was 39, uh, roles were made redundant or were changed as a result of the review.

Please, you're welcome to correct me if I'm wrong. I think it's page 47 of the, um, sessions to the committee. So what was the total cost of the restructure, and can you break and how much was the redundancy payments paid out to staff? Yeah, I can, I can answer that. So, um, obviously in our, uh, annual report and in our questions, we were reporting that as of 30 June, our restructure actually spanned through to the 13th of October.

So if I may, I'll use the, the total numbers that we reached as Of October. Okay. We're gonna, we're gonna go to a primary Oh, oh, sorry. Yeah. So that was, uh, there were 68 redundancies, um, completed by 13th of October at a cost of $2.6 million in those redundancies.

Okay. Thank you. My chance, uh, Chloe Sawman, Thank you very much, um, Moreno and welcome, uh, to the role. Mm-hmm. I guess I had wanted to tease out a little bit of what Dr. Deborah Russell was alluding to, and it should actually just be put on record for everybody, that we did actually ask the former Reserve Bank governor at the time that the dual mandate was whittled down to that one mandate that RBNZ told us that that actually wasn't gonna change all too much practically in terms of how you approached the role.

Yeah. Can I just confirm that that is still the case? It's an economic question. I think it's impossible to just consider inflation in isolation without thinking about employment Conditions. I guess further to that point, over the last few years, and FEC, particularly during COVID and through unconventional monetary policy, uh, tried to raise the alarm about distributional impacts of particularly unconventional monetary policy without, uh, intervening or mitigating fiscal policy.

Uh, I guess as far as that goes, is RBNZ still going to be concerning itself with distributional impacts with particularly monetary policy? And what can we expect that to look like moving forward, especially under new leadership? Should I answer? Yeah. No, but I think it's important that we look at, Uh, distribution of incomes, of course.

Mm-hmm. But the mandate of the rrb NZ, is to keep inflation low and stable. And we have to remember that high inflation hurts everyone, but it particularly hurts households, low incomes. Yeah. And there's a lot of research on this, and you can look at the data, and it's also true that inflation is high.

It eats real wages for households, and that means that they will spend less, and that means that the economy will perform less, and it means that employment growth will also be weaker. So that's why I think it's reasonable for us to focus on our mandate. Mm-hmm. But taking into consideration, of course, how the labor market evolves.

Mm-hmm. But in the long run, you will not have a strong labor market unless inflation is low and stable. And I guess, and please just please remember how hurtful high inflation is for households. Totally. And I guess as a kind of counterfactual, but a very live example of that, I mean, a ruthless application of that sole mandate and of taking inflation low was, uh, expressed in the sentiments of, uh, the former Reserve Bank Governor when under questioning from me at the end of 2022, he said that the RBNZ was trying

to manufacture a recession in order to bring down inflation. Yeah. Um, is that a kind of frankness that we can expect from you? I think the Reserve Bank, uh, under my leadership will always know exactly what our mandate is, but we will have a view over the economy that we aim to support a healthy and strong and growing economy, but keeping inflation low and stable in that economy.

I guess to that effect, uh, with fresh ice, uh, you may be aware of the fact that house prices have been quite a substantial topic, uh, particularly for this committee and when we are looking at the impacts of monetary policy, but also obviously, uh, fiscal policy as well. So how do you conceive of the role or the place of the housing market and the ostensible recovery that we're currently undertaking? Yeah.

Uh, I have, uh, looked at all the data, of course. Um, and house prices did grow considerably during the COVID period, and then they fallen. It's different across different parts of the country now. It's stabilizing somewhat. It is very important to household in terms of their wealth, in terms of construction.

So it has a large impact on the New Zealand economy. Ideally, you would want less substantial swings in house prices over time, and that construction needs better meets the de demand of households. But we know that this is complicated and difficult. It is in many countries, uh, but it does have an, a large impact on the economy, how, how prices involve.

Mm-hmm. And that's something obviously that the Reserve Bank is following. Okay. Final Up. Yeah, I mean, I guess part of what my line of questioning is trying to underscore is the ongoing challenge that we have, particularly as a committee, to try and communicate, especially to members of the public, how blunt an instrument monetary policy is, uh, and what the role of fiscal policy is, particularly when it comes to softening, um, you know, those distributional impacts and otherwise.

So what I'm trying to get out of you is just some articulation of how frank and how clear we can expect that you will be about the limitations of monetary policy and potentially where, I don't know, government has to take responsibility. Well, I really think that one of my roles together with everyone at the Reserve Bank is to focus on, uh, explaining our core mandate, but also being very clear in our communication.

So I will take what you're saying with me, and I will take that into the next year and look at, you know, both in terms of the reset that we do, but also how we communicate to the wider public, because it is important. Thank you. Okay. Uh, thank you. Now I'm gonna go to Nancy Lou for primary. I've got a lot of primaries here, so we, um, push as many through as we can. Okay. Uh, I'll group all my primary and supplementary into one question then.

Um, this is about the workforce. Um, just noting, uh, the the current structure and all that, but also noting, um, the, at the height in March, 2025, at about 778 in head count, that is increased almost 400% in the last 10 years, or over 200% doubled in the last five years.

Even with the restructure of happening at the moment, we are still very, very large in terms of headcount numbers, which means salary costs, which means operational costs for entire bank. So beyond the operational savings that we are achieving with the restructuring, what are the benefits and values that the bank will achieve from the restructuring with better productivity, better efficiency, new ways of working?

And maybe potentially even just slowing down a little bit more Quick question, but No, no, it is big. So I'll start at the beginning and, and the beginning is when one talks about our current headcount level is large. Mm-hmm. It's in contrast to what Yeah. And, and, and I always, you know, as I speak to people, remind that Cosby New Zealand are a small country.

We have this beautiful thing called a universal central bank. We do it all. We do what a PRA does, what the Reserve Bank of Australia does. Mm-hmm. What the insurance council, you know, we, we regulate insurance banks money, we money in cash and society, it financial policy, monetary policy, financial stability.

It's a huge universe of work. Um, so when we look at a broader and start combining an A PRA with an RBA or a, it doesn't look such a big number. Implicit in your question though, is, you know, why has it gone up? Respect your number 400%. Um, the bank is doing a lot more, Ms.

Luke, you know, uh, all our work on the new Deposit takers act was all new deposit compensation scheme from scratch. We never had one. We were one of the few OECD nations that did not protect depositors. Mm-hmm. Um, that comes with, with, with headcount. Finally, I say, uh, you know, super question, uh, efficiency.

It's now in our remit. It's, you know, uh, uh, do we have regard to the remit? Of course we do. You know, some people say, do you have regard to the remit? Or it's just a remit. You know, we think about the remit and every board paper in every discussion, we think about that remit and efficiency is buried in the remit. And we will do everything in our power with our reduced resources to be efficient.

Indeed, the board on the 11th of December is receiving a whole new piece of work on our ways of working and embedding further efficiencies into our organization. Will that be made available publicly? Um, you know, we don't have plans to release that particular paper, but it's certainly something that we can, we can start to talk about more.

So people have an idea of good where and how we're gonna get these efficiency measures in our organization. Ask Us up on that. Okay. Up from, So, so around efficiency savings, um, just noting that the total workforce head count for your enterprise services has had a 48% reduction, I just wanna make sure, like, so what are you, what are your strategies to ensure that that risk of that quite a large reduction, um, and enterprise services won't put anything else at risk in relation to the analysis or the systems that the bank uses

to analyze the data that it needs to do? Well, You've got the person per perfect person here. Yeah. So I, I'm the assistant governor of the enterprise area. So what we are is we're a collection of essentially corporate services across the bank, covering things like legal, ministerials, comms strategy, accountability. So, you know, really the backbone of the organization as, uh, my colleague explained, uh, we've done a targeted approach to reducing where our headcount is.

So our operational side, the bank has reduced the most, but what we've done is do it in a targeted way. So we really are clear and ensure that we meet the requirements of each of the important functions that sit under these banners. So, you know, we're confident that we've got those rights so that yes, they are reduced, but they still are going to meet the expectations of each function.

So have you now got actual full-time employees in your ministerial services team, noting that earlier in the year you were using contractors? Yep. So we do have some full-time employees and we are still using some contractors. The shape of that chain team has changed quite a bit.
